summary: A LinkedIn profile for Christopher Manning detailing his publications and projects in natural language processing, including work on machine translation evaluation, unlexicalized parsing, and on-the-job learning.

principles.statement: Machine translation evaluation metrics must capture semantic meaning, not just surface formulation.
principles.rationale: Traditional metrics struggle to distinguish good from fair translations because they ignore whether the candidate actually means the same thing as the reference.
principles.source_quote: A good translation candidate _means_ the same thing as the reference translation, regardless of formulation.

principles.statement: High-accuracy classification systems can be deployed without massive up-front data labeling.
principles.rationale: By using an 'on-the-job' approach with real-time crowdsourcing to resolve uncertainty, models can improve over time while balancing latency, cost, and accuracy.
principles.source_quote: We consider an "on-the-job" setting, where as queries arrive, we use real-time crowdsourcing to resolve uncertainty where needed and output our classification when confident.

principles.statement: Unlexicalized parsing can achieve high accuracy by breaking down false independence assumptions.
principles.rationale: Applying simple, linguistically motivated state splits to a vanilla treebank grammar significantly improves parsing accuracy.
principles.source_quote: We demonstrate that an unlexicalized PCFG can parse much more accurately than previously shown, by making use of simple, linguistically motivated state splits, which break down false independence assumptions latent in a vanilla treebank grammar

signature_quotes.text: Current evaluation metrics for machine translation have increasing difficulty in distinguishing good from merely fair translations. We believe the main problem to be their inability to properly capture _meaning_.
signature_quotes.context: From the abstract of his 2009 paper on measuring machine translation quality.

anti_patterns.description: Evaluating machine translation quality without capturing semantic equivalence.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to an inability to distinguish good translations from merely fair ones, as it focuses on formulation rather than meaning.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on vanilla treebank grammars with false independence assumptions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It limits parsing accuracy, which can be overcome by using simple, linguistically motivated state splits.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming an enormous up-front data labelling effort is required to deploy a high-accuracy, low-latency classification system.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the viability of 'on-the-job' learning, where real-time crowdsourcing can resolve uncertainty dynamically as queries arrive.
